payment_terms.summary: You'll be paid $150/hour, which is good. But invoices are paid net-45 — meaning you wait 45 days after submitting each invoice. For a freelancer billing monthly, you could go nearly 75 days between starting work and getting paid. Late payments do accrue 1% monthly interest, giving you some protection. There's also a 15-day dispute window where payment can be held up.

key_clauses.title: Net-45 Payment Terms
key_clauses.text: Client shall pay all undisputed invoices within 45 days of receipt.

key_clauses.title: Late Payment Interest
key_clauses.text: Late payments shall accrue interest at 1% per month.

recommendations: Negotiate to net-30 (industry standard) or net-15 — every day matters for cash flow as a freelancer.
Ask for an upfront deposit (25-30%) before starting work, especially for projects longer than 2 weeks.
Clarify what makes an invoice 'disputed' — the 15-day window is vague and could be abused to delay payment.

ip_ownership.summary: This is a complete IP giveaway. Everything you create — code, designs, documentation, even ideas and inventions — becomes the client's sole property. You cannot show any of your work in your portfolio without their written permission. This is unusually restrictive. Most freelancer agreements at least allow portfolio use, and many limit IP assignment to final deliverables only, not all work-in-progress and ideas.

key_clauses.title: Full IP Assignment — Everything You Create
key_clauses.text: All work product, code, documentation, designs, inventions, and any other materials created by Contractor in connection with the Services shall be the sole and exclusive property of Client. Contractor hereby assigns to Client all right, title, and interest in and to the Work Product, including all intellectual property rights therein.

key_clauses.title: No Portfolio Rights
key_clauses.text: Contractor retains no rights to use the Work Product for any purpose, including portfolio display, without Client's prior written consent.

key_clauses.title: Duty to Sign Further Documents
key_clauses.text: Contractor agrees to execute any further documents reasonably requested by Client to perfect such assignment.

recommendations: Absolutely negotiate for portfolio rights — at minimum, the right to show anonymized work samples. This is standard in the industry and costs the client nothing.

Narrow IP assignment to 'final deliverables accepted and paid for by Client' rather than 'any and all materials created in connection with the Services' — this prevents them from claiming ownership of your rough drafts, rejected ideas, or tools you build for yourself.
Consider retaining ownership of pre-existing materials, tools, and general know-how you bring to the project.

non_compete.summary: This is the most dangerous clause in the contract. You are banned from working for any business that 'competes with Client's primary business of enterprise SaaS solutions, anywhere in the United States' for 12 months after this contract ends. 'Enterprise SaaS' is an enormous category — this could block you from working for thousands of companies across the entire country. You also cannot hire or solicit any of the client's employees or contractors for 24 months.

key_clauses.title: 12-Month Nationwide Non-Compete
key_clauses.text: During the term of this Agreement and for a period of 12 months following termination, Contractor shall not, directly or indirectly, provide services that are competitive with the Services to any business that competes with Client's primary business of enterprise SaaS solutions, anywhere in the United States.

key_clauses.title: 24-Month Non-Solicitation
key_clauses.text: Contractor shall not solicit or hire any Client employees or contractors for 24 months following termination.

recommendations: Push back hard on this clause — it could prevent you from earning a living for a full year. Ask to remove it entirely or at minimum: (a) narrow 'enterprise SaaS' to the client's specific product category, (b) reduce geographic scope to the client's actual market, (c) reduce duration to 3-6 months.
Note: if you're in California, non-competes for independent contractors are generally unenforceable. But don't sign it hoping a court will save you — the legal fight itself is expensive.
The non-solicitation is more reasonable but 24 months is unusually long — push for 12 months, which is the standard.

termination.summary: This is actually one of the fairer termination clauses you'll see. Either party can end the contract with just 15 days notice. If the client fires you without cause, you get a 2-week termination fee as compensation. There's no auto-renewal trap. You get paid for all work completed up to termination. This is freelancer-friendly.

key_clauses.title: Termination Fee for No-Cause Termination
key_clauses.text: In the event of termination by Client without cause, Client shall pay Contractor a termination fee equal to two weeks of estimated hours at the agreed rate.

key_clauses.title: 15-Day Notice Period
key_clauses.text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time, with or without cause, upon 15 days written notice.

recommendations: Clarify what 'estimated hours' means for the termination fee — is it based on your average weekly hours over the past month? Or a fixed number? Get this in writing.
Consider asking for 30 days notice instead of 15 for more runway — 15 days is short if you need to line up your next client.
No changes needed on the core terms — this is a good clause.

indemnification.summary: You agree to cover ALL of the client's costs and losses — including their attorney fees — if anything goes wrong. This covers three things: (1) you breaching the contract (fair), (2) your negligence or misconduct (fair), and (3) any claim that your work infringes someone else's IP. That third one is dangerous — if a patent troll sues the client over something in your code, you pay the client's legal bills AND the settlement. The indemnification is completely one-sided — the client doesn't indemnify you for anything, not even for materials or instructions they provide.

key_clauses.title: One-Sided Indemnification Including IP Claims
key_clauses.text: Contractor agrees to indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Client, its officers, directors, employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, damages, losses, liabilities, costs, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising out of or related to: ... any claim that the Work Product infringes any third-party intellectual property rights.

recommendations: Add a 'knowledge qualifier' to the IP indemnity: only indemnify for infringement claims based on code you knowingly copied, not for independent creation that happens to overlap with a patent.
Make indemnification mutual — the client should indemnify you for things they provide (specs, data, materials, access) that cause problems.
Consider professional liability (E&O) insurance if you're taking on this kind of risk — it typically costs $50-100/month for freelancers.

liability_caps.summary: On the surface, your liability is capped at what the client paid you in the last 6 months — that's standard and fair. BUT there are three gaping exceptions: breaches of confidentiality, indemnification obligations, and IP infringement claims. For those three things, your liability is UNLIMITED. Combined with the broad indemnification clause (which already exposes you to IP claims), this means a single lawsuit could exceed everything you've ever earned from this client — potentially by millions.

key_clauses.title: Uncapped Liability for Key Areas
key_clauses.text: EXCEPT FOR BREACHES OF CONFIDENTIALITY, INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS, AND INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY INFRINGEMENT, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES.

key_clauses.title: 6-Month Fee Cap for Everything Else
key_clauses.text: CONTRACTOR'S TOTAL AGGREGATE LIABILITY TO CLIENT SHALL NOT EXCEED THE TOTAL FEES RECEIVED BY CONTRACTOR FROM CLIENT IN THE 6 MONTHS PRECEDING THE CLAIM.

recommendations: Negotiate a super-cap that covers everything including the exceptions — e.g., 'Contractor's total liability under any theory shall not exceed 2x the total fees paid in the 12 months preceding the claim.' This is a common compromise.
If the client won't budge on uncapped IP liability, require them to carry IP insurance that names you as an additional insured.
At an absolute minimum, add a carve-out that says you're not liable for IP claims arising from materials, designs, or instructions provided by the client.
